title: Quantum Ergodicity and Thermalization in Interval Quantum Mechanics
abstract: We combine Reimann's spectral typicality theorem -- a modern formulation of quantum ergodicity -- with the framework of Interval Quantum Mechanics (IQM). In IQM, quantum states are represented not by points but by \emph{quantum parcels}: weak open convex sets of density matrices defined by finitely many expectation intervals. Such parcels are the exact mathematical representation of the epistemic knowledge obtained from finite-precision measurements of macroscopic observables.   We prove that for a single parcel in which every state has large effective dimension (a condition that ensures thermalization), the expectation interval of any bounded observable becomes concentrated around the microcanonical value for most late times. The asymptotic bound depends only on the minimal effective dimension within the parcel, not on its detailed shape.   For a double parcel \((O_1,O_2)\) with both components contained in an energy shell, separated by a conserved quantity \(Q^*\) that is supported on the range of the measurement projector, we show that the expectation intervals of both parcels become concentrated near the microcanonical values of bounded observables, the separation is preserved exactly, and the updated double parcel after a fuzzy measurement remains valid.
summary: This paper develops a framework using Interval Quantum Mechanics (IQM) to analyze quantum thermalization. It shows that when the system's knowledge is represented by a 'quantum parcel,' the expectation values of observables converge to microcanonical values, provided the parcel has sufficient effective dimension. The method is particularly strong for tracking multiple, separated components of the state simultaneously.
main_problem: The paper combines quantum ergodicity theorems with Interval Quantum Mechanics (IQM) to rigorously study how quantum states, represented by epistemic 'quantum parcels,' approach thermal equilibrium.
main_result: It proves that for parcels with sufficient effective dimension, the expectation values of observables concentrate around the microcanonical ensemble values for late times, and this concentration is preserved even when tracking separated state components.
method: The analysis uses Reimann's spectral typicality theorem within the IQM framework, treating quantum states as convex sets defined by measurement intervals.
model_or_system: The system is a general quantum mechanical system governed by a Hamiltonian H. The state is not a point but a 'quantum parcel' ($\mathcal{O}$), which is a weak open convex set of density matrices reflecting finite-precision measurement knowledge.
key_observables: Expectation intervals of observables, effective dimension ($	ext{deff}$), and the separation maintained by a conserved quantity $Q^*$.
important_parameters: The minimal effective dimension $D$ (required to ensure thermalization) and the time $t$ (late times).
main_assumptions: The analysis requires the initial state parcel to have a large effective dimension ($	ext{deff} \geq D \gg 1$) to ensure the system is in the thermalizing regime.
paper_structure: The paper builds from establishing the general framework of IQM for quantum states, applying Reimann's theorem to show single-parcel thermalization, and culminating in the proof for double parcels, which maintains both thermalization and separation under evolution.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, geometrically rigorous tool for studying thermalization in open or partially known quantum systems, moving beyond point-state assumptions common in standard quantum dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
